questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Java hashCode/equals contract — what must you recite? Which response is most accurate?
options: Equal objects must share hashCode; unequal may collide; mutate keys carefully
equals can ignore hashCode entirely with no rules
hashCode must be unique for every object
hashCode may change freely after insert into a HashMap
questions.explanation: Consistency between equals and hashCode is mandatory for hash-based collections.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Average vs worst lookup in a hash map? Which response is most accurate?
options: Average O(1) expected with good hash/load; worst can degrade (long chains / bad hash)
questions.explanation: Amortized/expected constant; adversarial or poor hashing hurts.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Chaining vs open addressing — one-line distinction? Which response is most accurate?
options: Open addressing needs linked lists per key
Chaining stores collisions in buckets (lists/trees); open addressing probes other slots in the table
questions.explanation: Two classic collision resolutions.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Load factor — what is it and why resize? Which response is most accurate?
options: A synonym for collision count exactly
n/capacity; beyond a threshold, rehash to keep operations fast
questions.explanation: Keep density low enough for short probes/chains.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: When does a hash map beat sorting for 'unique elements' / frequency? Which response is most accurate?
options: When you need expected O(n) counts/membership vs O(n log n) sort
questions.explanation: Hashing trades memory for expected linear passes.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Mutable object as HashMap key — danger? Which response is most accurate?
options: No danger if equals is overridden
Changing fields that affect hashCode/equals after insert loses the entry
questions.explanation: Key identity must remain stable while in the map.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Hash set vs hash map — screening difference? Which response is most accurate?
options: Set stores key→value; map stores only keys
Set is membership of keys; map associates keys to values (set often map to present flag)
Map forbids null in all languages always
questions.explanation: Set = keys only; map = key/value.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Why is 'O(1) hash map' a slight interview oversimplification? Which response is most accurate?
options: Hashing the key can be O(key size); constants and collisions matter; worst-case ≠ average
questions.explanation: Be precise: expected time, key-length cost, load factor.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Frequency multiset problem (count occurrences) — go-to structure? Which response is most accurate?
options: Raw array only when universe is unbounded 64-bit
questions.explanation: Counting map is the default multiset tool.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Perfect hashing — what does it mean informally? Which response is most accurate?
options: A hash scheme with no collisions for a fixed known key set
questions.explanation: Static sets can get collision-free layouts.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Two Sum with hash map — what do you store? Which response is most accurate?
options: Seen values → indices; probe for complement
questions.explanation: Complement lookup while scanning.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Bucket index from hash — typical step? Which response is most accurate?
options: Use hash % capacity (or bit mask when capacity is power of two)
questions.explanation: Map hash space onto table slots.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Collision — definition? Which response is most accurate?
options: Different keys map to the same bucket/slot index
equals returns true for identical references only
questions.explanation: Same slot, different keys. State this clearly and mention complexity when relevant.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: TreeMap vs HashMap — when mention TreeMap? Which response is most accurate?
options: When you need average O(1) and unordered keys
When you need sorted keys / range queries; operations O(log n)
questions.explanation: Ordered map vs unordered hash map.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: IdentityHashMap / reference equality — when? Which response is most accurate?
options: To fix hashCode contract violations automatically
When key identity is == not equals (rare framework cases)
questions.explanation: Reference equality is a specialized need.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Caching function results (memoization) — structure? Which response is most accurate?
questions.explanation: Memo tables are hash maps (or arrays when domain is dense).

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Detect cycle in linked list alternatives — hashing approach? Which response is most accurate?
options: Store seen node identities in a hash set while walking
Hash the integer values only (always correct with duplicates)
questions.explanation: Seen-set works; Floyd is O(1) space alternative.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Why redistribute on resize? Which response is most accurate?
options: To shuffle user data randomly for security theater only
Capacity change changes slot indices; entries must be re-placed under new mask/modulo
questions.explanation: New table size ⇒ new bucket indices.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Hash multiset removal — detail to get right? Which response is most accurate?
options: Always leave count at zero forever
Delete all keys on one remove
Decrement count; remove key when count hits zero
questions.explanation: Zero-count keys should disappear to keep maps clean.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Screening: expected time to check key membership in a well-sized hash set? Which answer is best?
questions.explanation: Expected constant-time membership.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Subarray sum equals k with negatives — hash role? Which response is most accurate?
options: Map prefixSum → frequency; look for prefixSum−k
questions.explanation: Prefix frequencies turn the problem into complement counts.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Group anagrams — hashing angle? Which response is most accurate?
options: Hash canonical signature (sorted string or count tuple) to list
questions.explanation: Signature hashing buckets equivalents.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: LRU cache — structures you combine? Which response is most accurate?
options: Hash map key→node plus doubly linked list (or LinkedHashMap ordering)
questions.explanation: O(1) lookup + O(1) splice for recency.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Copy list with random pointer — hash trick? Which response is most accurate?
options: Hash only the random indices as ints
Map original node → clone while first pass, wire next/random second pass
questions.explanation: Identity map from old to new nodes.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Longest consecutive sequence — why hash set? Which response is most accurate?
options: O(1) predecessor checks; only expand from streak starts
Sets keep order of insertion as sorted order
questions.explanation: Membership tests make linear total expansion.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Four sum II style — hashing optimization? Which response is most accurate?
options: Hash sums of one pair of arrays; probe complements from the other pair
Sort all four and binary search once
questions.explanation: Meet-in-the-middle via sum frequencies.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Isomorphic strings — map usage? Which response is most accurate?
options: Bijection maps s→t and t→s (or map + used-set) character-wise
questions.explanation: Need both directions to enforce one-to-one mapping.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Word pattern ('abba' vs 'dog cat cat dog') — same idea as? Which response is most accurate?
options: Isomorphic mapping between pattern tokens and words
questions.explanation: Token isomorphism via dual maps.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Design a sparse vector dot product — structure? Which response is most accurate?
options: Hash map index→value for nonzeros; iterate smaller side probing the other
questions.explanation: Store nonzeros; intersect indices efficiently.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Find duplicate files by content — hashing approach? Which response is most accurate?
options: Compare all pairs of full files always first
Hash content (often hierarchical: size then hash) to group candidates, then verify
questions.explanation: Content hashes cluster likely duplicates.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Rolling hash for substring search — update idea? Which response is most accurate?
options: Recompute from scratch each window always
Remove leaving char contribution, shift, add entering char — O(1) per slide
Use equals on whole text each step
questions.explanation: Algebraic update makes Rabin–Karp slide fast.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Count distinct elements in every window of size k — tools? Which response is most accurate?
options: Sliding window + hash map of frequencies; bump distinct when count transitions 0↔1
questions.explanation: Freq map tracks how many unique in window.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Alien dictionary order — hashing/graph combo? Which response is most accurate?
options: Build adjacency from sorted adjacent word diffs; then Kahn/DFS topo; map for edges
HashSet of letters only solves order
questions.explanation: Edge set from mismatches + topological sort.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Randomized Set insert/remove/getRandom O(1) — layout? Which response is most accurate?
options: ArrayList of values + HashMap value→index; swap-remove on delete
questions.explanation: Swap-with-last + index map is the classic design.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Two strings are permutations — hash way without sorting? Which response is most accurate?
questions.explanation: Equal counts ⇔ same multiset of chars.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Avoiding DoS from hash collisions in servers — mitigation talk track? Which response is most accurate?
options: Always use open addressing without probes
Sort every map key on insert only
SipHash/randomized seeds, treeify long buckets, limits on request maps
questions.explanation: Adversarial keys can force O(n) chains without defenses.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Consistent hashing — problem it targets? Which response is most accurate?
options: Distributed key remapping: minimize key movement when nodes add/remove
questions.explanation: Ring hashing for caches/shards.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Bloom filter — what do you promise? Which response is most accurate?
options: Probabilistic set: no false negatives; possible false positives; tiny space
questions.explanation: Space-efficient approximate membership.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: First missing positive without huge array — hashing angle vs index-hash? Which answer is best?
options: Must allocate HashSet of size Integer.MAX_VALUE
HashSet of nums then scan 1..n+1, or cycle-sort index placement for O(1) extra
questions.explanation: Set scan is simple; in-place index map is the optimization.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Map of maps for prefix frequencies of pairs — when? Which response is most accurate?
options: Problems needing counts of pairs/tuples (e.g. 4Sum variants, geometric pairs)
questions.explanation: Nested frequency tables appear in pair-counting problems.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Senior: Java 8+ HashMap treeifies long colliding bins — why? Which response is most accurate?
options: Bound worst-case lookup toward O(log n) under collision attacks / bad hashes
Trees use less memory than lists always
questions.explanation: Tree bins mitigate pathological chains.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Open addressing deletion pitfall? Which response is most accurate?
options: Just clear the slot to empty always works with linear probe
Must use tombstones (or careful rehash) so probe sequences don't break
questions.explanation: Empty holes truncate probes incorrectly without tombstones.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Why power-of-two capacities in many hash maps? Which response is most accurate?
options: Allow bit-mask instead of modulo; faster index from hash
questions.explanation: index = hash & (cap−1) when cap is 2^k.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: hashCode quality: only using one field of a multi-field key? Which response is most accurate?
options: Increases collisions when that field repeats; mix all significant fields
questions.explanation: Poor hash distribution destroys performance.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Memory: HashMap overhead vs dense array for int→int on 0..n? Which response is most accurate?
options: HashMap has per-entry object overhead; dense array often wins for dense universes
Overhead is exactly 1 bit per key
questions.explanation: Constants and object headers matter at scale.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: ConcurrentHashMap vs synchronized HashMap — interview point? Which response is most accurate?
options: CHM allows finer-grained concurrency; don't iterate shared HashMap without external sync
CHM permits mutable keys safely always
questions.explanation: Concurrency story beats 'just synchronize everything' handwaving.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: When sort+two-pointers beats hash for pairs? Which response is most accurate?
options: Tight memory, need deterministic O(n log n), or avoid hash overhead/worst-case concerns
When keys are strings of length 10^6 each
questions.explanation: Sorting can be the better engineering choice.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Floating-point keys in hash maps — caution? Which response is most accurate?
options: Floats hash perfectly with no surprises
Floats cannot be hashed in Java
Use == instead of equals for all floats in sets
NaN/−0.0 equals quirks; prefer canonicalization or avoid as keys
questions.explanation: IEEE edge cases break naive keying.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Universal hashing — why mention theoretically? Which response is most accurate?
options: Randomized hash families bound expected collisions vs adversarial keys
It guarantees perfect hashing for streaming infinite sets
It replaces cryptographic hashes in TLS
questions.explanation: Expected-case guarantees against worst inputs.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Resizing storms under load — operational pitfall? Which response is most accurate?
options: Sudden large rehash can stall latency; size capacity / use incremental strategies when relevant
questions.explanation: Stop-the-world rehash is a real latency spike.
